FRANCESCO ROMANO: Hey everyone!

Do you remember when

external displays.

We're using styluses for

and trackpads for
nuanced control.

The interaction model has
grown far beyond a single touch
on a small screen.

Today, users are expecting

devices, especially
on this larger form factors.

Users aren't just consuming
content on these larger screens.

They want to create, to
work, to be productive.

And they expect desktop
level interactions.

This is why Android is
investing in desktop windowing.

that it will be included in

Release.

In this case, I pick
single instance per task.

This activity can

And therefore, there
can only be one instance

Remember that a new task

will always result

You can now start multiple
instances of your activity

by adding those
flags in the intent.

When using the new
task flag alone,

if a task is already
running for the activity

you are now starting, then a new

Instead, the current

be brought to the

If the flag is paired with
flag activity multiple task,

then it will skip the

DRAG FLAG GLOBAL

DRAG FLAG START INTENT

Desktop windowing

25 Q3, as a developer preview,

experience will

When the phone is connected

the phone state

The phone and the external

And apps are specific

When a desktop windowing-enabled

They then function as

Let's talk about
some best practices

to ensure a smooth and
high-quality user experience.

First, do not assume the display
object will remain constant.

When a user connects or
disconnects an external monitor

or even moves your app
window between displays,

the underlying display object
associated with your apps,
context, or window can change.

Its properties like size,
resolution, refresh rate,

and density can
all be different.

Hardcoding values based
on the phone screen,

for example, will break
layouts on an external display.

Second, you must account for
density configuration changes.

External monitors can
have vastly different
pixel densities.

So make sure your app
responds correctly
to these density changes.

This means using
density-independent pixels

for layout, providing
density-specific resources,

and ensuring your UI
scales appropriately.

Finally, think beyond
just the screen.

When users connect to
an external monitor,

they often create a more
desktop-like environment.

This frequently involves using
external keyboards, mice,

trackpads, webcams,
microphones, and speakers.

So ensure your app
works seamlessly
with these peripherals.

Handle keyboard shortcuts,
mouse pointer interactions,

correctly support external
cameras or microphones,

and respect audio
output routing.
So what's the next step?

The great news is that enhanced
support for external monitors,

including many of the
capabilities we have discussed

today, is coming soon as a

beta program.

Stay tuned to learn how to

experience.

This is the perfect

Remember that building apps that

MIGUEL MONTEMAYOR:
Thanks, Francesco.

Styluses are transforming
Android devices

into powerful tools for both
productivity and creativity.

Users expect inking to
feel as natural and fluid
as writing on paper.

To create a simple, fluid
inking experience like this,

it generally required
a lot of investment

in graphics and geometry
just to get started.

Developers had to
handle everything

from generating stroke
shapes with stylus input,

to rendering those strokes
quickly and efficiently,

and even performing complex
geometric calculations

for things like
erasing and selection.

We built the ink Jetpack
library to simplify

the process of creating,
rendering, and manipulating
beautiful ink strokes.

It provides a powerful
and modular toolkit

for integrating rich inking
features into your app.

The library consists
of six modules.

The Strokes module is the
foundation of the ink library,

handling core data types for
interacting with ink input.

The Authoring module
manages the capture

of user input and real
time rendering of strokes

and facilitates
low-latency rendering.

The Brush module manages
the visual style of strokes

through properties like
color, size, and texture.

The Geometry module provides
geometric primitives

for shape manipulation
and enables features

like stroke erasing
and selection.

The Rendering
module incorporates

canvasStrokeRenderer and
viewStrokeRenderer, offering

performant rendering pipelines
with anti-aliasing support

for Compose and
view-based UIs. Finally,

the Storage module
provides efficient storage
of stroke input data.

As a developer, you can
use only the modules needed
for your app's architecture.

We've received great feedback
from our early partners.

Ink API significantly reduced
the workload required for them

to implement responsive
fluid inking in their app.

Now let's take a look
at the Brush module

and some of the
recent improvements we
made with custom brushes.

You can use the Brush module
